coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical detection of riboflavin using tin-chitosan modified pencil graphite electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Nagarajan, Sedhu
Vairamuthu, Raj
coredata.dc:description: We here report a simple fabrication of tin incorporated chitosan polymer matrix coated pencil graphite electrode Sn/Cs/PGE for the electrochemical detection of riboflavin (RF). The tin incorporated chitosan polymer was prepared by mixing tin chloride in an aqueous acidic chitosan solution and the fabrication of Sn/Cs/PGE was done by simple immersion method for the first time. The surface characterization of the electrode sensor was carried out by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), scanning electron microscope (SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy and elemental mapping analysis. Electrochemical characterization was performed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using 0.1 M KCl containing K3[Fe(CN)6]. These results revealed that the Sn/Cs/PGE shows a sensitive and fast electrocatalytic response towards the sensing of RF in physiological pH with a wide linear range (10 to 1200 nM) and low limit of detection (5.56 nM) compared to other electrodes. Moreover, the Sn/Cs/PGE possesses better stability with the anti-inference ability and the practical feasibility of the fabricated electrode shows satisfactory results towards the medicinal and food analysis.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical detection of riboflavin using tin-chitosan modified pencil graphite electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Materials Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Periyar University, Salem 636011, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Materials Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Periyar University, Salem 636011, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We here report a simple fabrication of tin incorporated chitosan polymer matrix coated pencil graphite electrode Sn/Cs/PGE for the electrochemical detection of riboflavin (RF). The tin incorporated chitosan polymer was prepared by mixing tin chloride in an aqueous acidic chitosan solution and the fabrication of Sn/Cs/PGE was done by simple immersion method for the first time. The surface characterization of the electrode sensor was carried out by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), scanning electron microscope (SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy and elemental mapping analysis. Electrochemical characterization was performed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using 0.1 M KCl containing K3[Fe(CN)6]. These results revealed that the Sn/Cs/PGE shows a sensitive and fast electrocatalytic response towards the sensing of RF in physiological pH with a wide linear range (10 to 1200 nM) and low limit of detection (5.56 nM) compared to other electrodes. Moreover, the Sn/Cs/PGE possesses better stability with the anti-inference ability and the practical feasibility of the fabricated electrode shows satisfactory results towards the medicinal and food analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Flavins are a kind of cyclic base pteridine with a tricyclic isoalloxazine core structure and one of the essential biologically active flavins is riboflavin (6,7-dimethyl 9-(D-1-ribityl)-isoalloxazine) [1]. Riboflavin (RF) is an important water-soluble vitamin (B2) and plays a vital role in the proper consumption of energy sources, such as proteins, fats and carbohydrates [2]. RF promotes cell growth and regeneration and it exists widely in food and medicinal products [3]. RF is a combination of flavin adenine dinucleotide and flavin nucleotide coenzymes, these two coenzymes are essential for proper tissue respiration and they are bounded with selective enzymes cause, reduction or oxidation by losing or gaining one or two protons or electrons [4]. Since the human body cannot be prepared RF on its own, it is necessary to take RF from milk, cheese, tea, egg, wine, vegetables and liver like nutritional supplements [5]. Though RF is essential for the regular metabolism of human growth, its deficiency may cause itching, irritation in the eye, sore tongue, stomatitis, hyperemia and other skin disorders [6–8]. Therefore, it is necessary to monitor the level of RF in food and pharmaceutical products. Nevertheless, the investigation on the inadequacy of RF is not shown much interest, particularly detection studies are very scarce. Till now, various analytical methods were utilized for the detection of RF, such as HPLC [9,10], liquid chromatography-diode array detection (LC–DAD) [11], fluorescence [12,13], capillary electrophoresis [14,15], spectrophotometry [16], chemiluminescence [17,18] and flow injection [19,20] analysis. However, these methods are too expensive, time-consuming, required skilled operators and tedious instrumental procedures, low sensitivity and selectivity [21–23]. Electroanalytical methods have recently been shown exceptional ability to overcome these limitations [24,25]. Since RF has excellent redox properties, the electroanalytical method is perfectly suited for the detection of RF [26]. So far, different electrochemical methods were used for the detection of RF, such as differential pulse voltammetry [27,28], square-wave adsorptive stripping voltammetry [29,30] and square wave voltammetry (SWV) [31]. All these electrochemical techniques required electrodes and modifications of the electrodes have to be carried out, for enhancing the sensitivity of the electrode towards the detection of RF. Some of the examples for the electrode modification towards the detection of RF are bismuth film [29], chromium doped tin oxide [32], nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/tin oxide nanocomposite [33], poly(glyoxal-bis(2-hydroxyanil))-amino-decorated graphene quantum dots-manganese dioxide [34] and carbon nanotube paste modified with anionic surfactant [35] electrodes. Most of these presented works used high-cost electrodes, modifiers, followed tedious pretreatment, multistep modification procedures and required careful storage for the modified electrodes. Recently, carbon-based electrodes [36–45], such as diamond [46], glassy carbon (GC) [47], carbon paste (CP) [48] and pencil graphite electrodes (PGE) [49] have got much attention, particularly PGE and modified PGE are utilized widely due to easy availability, reusability, stability, cost-effectiveness and facile modification process [50–52]. Modified PGEs have proved their better electrocatalytic efficiency towards various biological samples and determination of RF was also carried out with carbon nanotube coated PGE [53], DNA functionalized PGE [49] and Prussian blue-iron bipyridine hybrid modified PGE [54]. However, these methods consume a long time, costly and tedious fabrication procedure. In order to overcome these limitations, biocompatible chitosan (Cs) natural polymer was utilized for the fabrication of Cs/PGE by simple immersion technique.

Chitosan (Cs) has significant structural features, found in fungal cell walls and the exoskeleton of crustaceans contains a glucosamine residue with a primary amine at the C-2 position and is soluble in an aqueous solution of pH < 6.5 [55]. In Cs aqueous acidic solution, the amine groups are protonated (NH3 +) and carry a positive charge, thereby Cs can bind with negatively charged materials or surfaces [56]. Moreover, Cs have dramatic membrane forming ability, susceptibility, high mechanical strength, better adhesion, high water permeability, nontoxicity and biocompatibility, because of the active hydroxyl and amino groups [45–47]. Hence, Cs have been extensively utilized for various environmental applications. Further, different materials, such as nanoparticles, composites, metal complexes, carbon nanotubes and graphene oxides were dispersed in Cs polymer to increase its electronic conductivity and the modified Cs were applied for the electrochemical detection of various biological samples [57–59]. Tin is typical metal and in the solution of tin chloride, tin has a positive charge, so it can easily be incorporated in Cs polymeric structure thereby increasing the number of net electrons, charge carriers and electronic conductivity of the polymer [57,58,60]. In this present work, tin (Sn) incorporated Cs was prepared by simple magnetic stirring and coated on the PGE by electroless immersion deposition, then tin/chitosan-coated PGE (Sn/Cs/PGE) was dried in a vacuum desiccator for one hour.

To the best of our knowledge, the facile fabrication of Sn/Cs/PGE was constructed and applied for the electrochemical detection of RF in 0.1 M neutral phosphate buffer medium for the first time. The electrocatalytic ability of Sn/Cs/PGE towards the determination of RF was dramatically increased compared to that of bare PGE, this might be attributed to the increased net carrier charge and electronic conductivity of the coated chitosan polymer matrix incorporated with tin ions. Finally, the fabricated electrode was practically applied for the detection of RF in food and pharmaceutical products.

PGEs were acquired as pencil leads of type 2B with 0.5 mm and 60 mm of diameter and length, respectively from Kokuyo Camlin Ltd., Mumbai, India. chitosan, tin chloride, acetic acid, sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), potassium chloride (KCl) and K3[Fe(CN)6] were purchased from Merck. 0.2 M aqueous solution of phosphate buffer (PBS) prepared by sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) salts, the pH of PBS was adjusted by adding the required volumes of 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH solutions. All chemicals were of analytical grade, acquired from Merck and were utilized for the experiments without any pretreatment. All aqueous solutions used were prepared with triple distilled water.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed with a CHI760C electrochemical workstation, Austin, TX, USA. The electrochemical cell made up of a conventional three-electrode system, Ag/AgCl wire in 1.0 M KCl, a bright Platinum wire were used as reference and counter electrode, respectively. PGE and Sn/Cs/PGE were used as working electrode. All potentials presented in this paper were measured with respect to the Ag/AgCl in 1.0 M KCl reference electrode. The solutions were deoxygenated by purging high-purity of N2 prior to the each experiments and all the experiments were carried out at room temperature (28 °C). The SEM images, EDAX spectrum and elemental analysis of the various electrode surfaces were carried out using with Bruker nano, German. The fabrication of Sn/Cs/PGE and its electrochemical detection of RF is shown in Scheme 1 .

One end of the pencil lead of 0.5 mm diameter and 60 mm length was polished with emery papers of grade 800, 1200 and 2000, then with 0.05 µM of alumina slurry for a mirror like finishing. The chitosan, tin and tin incorporated chitosan modified pencil graphite electrodes (Cs/PGE, Sn/PGE and Sn/Cs/PGE) were prepared by the simple electroless deposition. The electroless immersion solution for Cs/PGE, Sn/PGE and Sn/Cs/PGE were prepared by mixing the 0.25 g chitosan in 0.2 M aqueous acetic acid solution (Cs/PGE), 40 mM of aqueous SnCl2 solution (Sn/PGE) and 40 mM of SnCl2 in 0.2 M aqueous acetic acid solution containing 0.25 g chitosan (Sn/Cs/PGE). All the immersion solutions were made up with triple distilled water for 25 mL and stirred for one hour with a magnetic stirrer. 1 cm of the polished end of the pencil lead was immersed in electroless deposition solution for 30 min then the electrodes were dried for 1 h before the electrochemical analysis, the other end of the electrode was wrapped by copper wire for electrical contact with the electrochemical workstation.

In order to find the effective tin concentration for the enhanced electrocatalytic sensing ability of the sensor, different concentrations of SnCl2 (20, 40, 60 and 80 mM) precursor were added in the chitosan polymer matrix. The modified electrodes were analyzed towards the reversible system of potassium hexacyanoferrate as a redox mediator in potassium chloride supporting electrolyte solution and the recorded CVs are shown in Fig. 1 . The CV curves of (a) to (d) in Fig. 1 are corresponding to the 20, 40, 60 and 80 mM of tin modified Sn/Cs/PGE electrodes. From Fig. 1 it was clearly shown that the 40 mM of tin incorporated chitosan-coated PGE has a better electrocatalytic effect towards Fe2+/3+ redox probe with reduced peak potential difference (ΔEp = 90 mV) compared to that of other concentration of tin incorporated chitosan modified electrodes. So, 40 mM of tin incorporated chitosan-coated PGE was chosen for the further preparation of Sn/Cs/PGE modified electrode.

The FTIR spectra of bare and modified electrodes are shown in Fig. 2 . The PGE spectrum shows characteristic peaks at 3729, 2884, 1499 and 1014 cm−1 corresponding to OH, CH, CC and CO groups [61], Cs/PGE contains two additional peaks at 2948 and 1592 cm−1 corresponding to the NH stretching and bending [61]. The FTIR spectra of Sn/PGE and Sn/Cs/PGE contain a new characteristic peak corresponding to the Sn–C group at 923 and 921 cm−1 [62]. These results support the successful formation of different coatings on the PGE surface during the electroless immersion technique.

The change in surface morphology and elemental composition of PGE during electroless deposition of various electrodes were investigated by scanning electron microscope (SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) and elemental mapping analyses. The SEM image shown in Fig. 3 A depicts the flaky surface of graphite (PGE), Fig. 3B and C show a covered flaky surface indicating chitosan polymer (Cs/PGE) and tin (Sn/PGE) deposition on the PGE surface, respectively. Fig. 3D shows a pipe-like shape indicates the tin incorporated chitosan polymer matrix coating on the PGE surface (Sn/Cs/PGE). Fig. 4 shows the EDX spectra of (A) PGE, (B) Cs/PGE, (C) Sn/PGE and (D) Sn/Cs/PGE. EDX spectrum of PGE shows only the carbon and oxygen elements, while Cs/PGE and Sn/PGE exhibit nitrogen and tin elements, respectively. On the other hand, Sn/Cs/PGE shows the presence of all the carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and tin elements with an increased percentage of oxygen, which supports the respective modification of the electrode. Elemental mappings of (A) PGE, (B) Cs/PGE, (C) Sn/PGE and (D) Sn/Cs/PGE are shown in Fig. 5 , which strongly supports the EDX spectrum response. These surface analysis results suggest that the formation of different modifications on the PGE surface by the simple immersion method.

The electrochemical properties of the bare and modified electrodes were characterized using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in potassium ferricyanide as redox probe in KCl supporting electrolyte. The cyclic voltammogram was recorded for bare and various modified electrodes in 0.1 M KCl containing 10 mM of K3[Fe(CN)6] and is shown in Fig. 6 . CV of Fe2+/3+ at (a) PGE, (b) Cs/PGE and (c) Sn/PGE was broad, confirming the low electrocatalytic ability of these electrodes towards the Fe2+/3+ probe molecule. The corresponding anodic and cathodic peak potentials for Fe2+/3+ at PGE are 0.34 and – 0.10 V with peak potential separation (ΔEp) of 440 mV, at Cs/PGE are 0.30 and – 0.70 V with 980 mV of ΔEp, at Sn/PGE are 0.41 and – 0.86 V with 410 mV of ΔEp. The redox peak currents of Fe2+/3+ at these electrodes were also very low; however, the redox peak currents of Fe2+/3+ at Sn/Cs/PGE are dramatically enhanced compared with other electrodes. The corresponding anodic and cathodic peak currents of Fe2+/3+ at Sn/Cs/PGE are six and four times higher than the unmodified PGE. Moreover, the redox peak potentials of Fe2+/3+ at Sn/Cs/PGE are 0.28 and 0.20 V with 80 mV of ΔEp, which is close to the theoretical value of 59 mV for the one-electron transfer mechanism for reversible systems. These results confirm that Sn/Cs/PGE modified electrode exhibits faster electron transfer kinetics and possesses a larger electroactive surface area compared to the PGE electrode. Moreover, chitosan polymer film provides the immobilization matrix for metal precursor by ion interaction and Sn/CS/PGE modified electrode was studied under different scan rates in 0.1 M KCl containing 10 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] is shown in Fig. 7 . The redox peak current of Fe2+/3+ increases with increasing the scan rate. Further, the electrocatalytic surface area of PGE, Cs/PGE, Sn/PGE and Sn/Cs/PGE were calculated using the Randles-Sevcik equation [63,64] (Eq. (1)).

where ‘ip’ is the peak current, ‘D’ is the diffusion coefficient (6.7 × 10−6 cm2 s−1 for [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−), ‘ν’ denotes sweep rate (0.05 V s−1), ‘A’ is the electroactive surface area, ‘C’ is the concentration of the redox probe (5 × 10−3 M) and ‘n’ is the count of electrons transferred during the redox process (n = 1 for Fe2+/3+ system). The corresponding electroactive surface areas calculated for PGE, Cs/PGE, Sn/PGE and Sn/Cs/PGE are 0.44, 0.50, 0.41 and 2.50 cm−2. The reduced peak potential separation and increased electroactive surface area confirm, the enhanced electrocatalytic behavior of the modified electrode (Sn/Cs/PGE) which might be attributed to the synergetic effect of tin incorporated chitosan coating on the PGE surface. Hence the fabricated electrode has the potential to be used for electrocatalytic sensing application.

The interfacial properties of the bare and modified (PGE, Cs/PGE, Sn/PGE and Sn/Cs/PGE) electrodes were investigated by using EIS experiments in 0.1 M KCl containing 10 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] as a redox probe at an applied potential of 0.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode. Fig. 8 A illustrates the comparative EIS responses (Nyquist plots) of bare and modified electrodes, thereby the information on the interfacial behavior of each modification on the PGE electrode can be obtained [65,66]. Fig. 8B shows the fitting equivalent circuit used to fit the EIS responses, the equivalent circuit consists of solution resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct) component phase element (CPE) and Warburg diffusion element (W). The obtained Rct value of PGE is about 4.21 KΩ, whereas the Rct of value Cs/PGE, Sn/PGE and Sn/Cs/PGE are about 0.16, 0.32 and 0.10 KΩ, showing a lower resistance at Cs/PGE Sn/PGE and Sn/Cs/PGE electrodes compared to that of the bare electrode, confirmed that an enhanced conductivity and faster electron transfer between the electrode surface and the redox probe. Further, Sn/Cs/PGE modified electrode shows almost a straight line with the lowest solution resistance because of better electronic conductivity and shorten thickness of a diffusional layer at the electrode/solution interface. The results derived from CV and EIS supports the reciprocal relationship between redox peak currents and charge transfer resistance that is enhanced peak current and reduced resistance. These results also support the tin incorporated chitosan coating on the PGE surface, increases the electroactive surface area thereby provide a mass transport channel between electrode and electrolyte interface for fast electron transfer kinetics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of RF at unmodified and modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of RF at unmodified and modified electrodes was investigated by using CV in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution of pH 7.0 at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1, in the (A) absence and (B) the presence of 2.0 µM of RF and the resulting CV are shown in Fig. 9 . CV of (a) PGE, (b) Cs/PGE, (c) Sn/PGE and (d) Sn/Cs/PGE in Fig. 9A shows no active redox peaks, suggesting the stability of electrodes in the recorded potential range. After the addition of 2.0 µM RF in 0.1 M PBS (Fig. 9B), PGE gives a broad redox peak for RF with a very low current response. However, Cs/PGE, Sn/PGE modified electrodes display visible redox peaks for RF, Sn/Cs/PGE modified electrode gives stable and well-defined anodic and cathodic peaks for RF. The corresponding redox peak potentials of RF at PGE is – 0.37 and – 0.48 V, at Cs/PGE is – 0.39 and – 0.47 V, at Sn/PGE is – 0.38 and – 0.48 V and at Sn/Cs/PGE is – 0.38 and – 0.45 V. The respective peak potential separations (ΔEp) at PGE, Cs/PGE, Sn/PGE and Sn/Cs/PGE are 110, 80, 100 and 70 mV, respectively. The redox peak currents of RF at Cs/PGE and Sn/PGE are higher than that of PGE. Moreover, the redox peak currents of RF at Sn/Cs/PGE modified electrode were enhanced dramatically and the peak potential separation also reduced much (40 mV) compared to that of the unmodified electrode. These results illustrate that the tin incorporated chitosan coating on the PGE surface enhanced the electrochemical sensing ability towards RF, which might be attributed to the increment in the electrochemical surface area and electronic conductivity of Sn/Cs/PGE modified electrode.

The electrochemical oxidation of RF at unmodified and modified electrodes was investigated by using SWV analysis in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.2 µM of RB and the resulting CV curves are shown in Fig. 10 . SWV responses are very much similar to that of CV results, in the absence of RF there is no characteristic peak appeared in the scanned potential range (Fig. 10A). After the addition of RF (Fig. 10B), a characterized oxidation peak appeared at each of the electrodes corresponding to the oxidation of RF. The respective anodic peak potential and current of RF are – 0.41 V and 0.76 µA at PGE, – 0.39 V and 1.08 µA at Cs/PGE, – 0.43 V and 3.28 µA at Sn/PGE and – 0.38 V and 15.15 µA at Sn/Cs/PGE. Further, the anodic peak potential of RF at Sn/Cs/PGE has shifted 30 mV towards less negative potential with twenty times of increased current response compared to that of the unmodified electrode. The oxidation of RF at Sn/Cs/PGE takes place with lesser energy with higher current sensitivity compared to that of bare and other modified electrodes, suggesting the better electrocatalytic sensing ability of Sn/Cs/PGE towards the electrochemical detection of RF.

pH is a significant parameter which affects the electrocatalytic sensing ability of electrode, which is used to predict the electrochemical bimolecular reaction pathway. The electrochemical redox properties of RF at Sn/Cs/PGE electrode were investigated in 0.1 M PBS at various pH from 3.0 to 9.0 containing 2.0 µM RF at the sweep rate of 50 mV s−1 and the responses were shown in Fig. 11 A. It is clearly observable from the CV plots that the redox peaks of RF are shifted towards more negative potential while increasing the pH from 3.0 to 9.0, confirms the protons participate in the electrocatalytic redox reaction of RF at Sn/Cs/PGE as shown in equation (2) [67].
(2)

H 2

Fig. 11B shows the linearity plot of Epa/c vs. pH, which confirms the better linearity between the peak potential and pH of the buffer solution with 0.9992 of correlation coefficient and the anodic peak potential of RF is given by the following equation (3) [67].
(3)

Here, Epa is the oxidation peak potential at pH = 0.0, ‘m’ denotes the number of protons that participated in the electrochemical reaction, R, T and F are constants and have their usual meanings. As shown in Fig. 11B, the Epa’ values are shifted linearly towards negative potentials while changing the buffer pH from 3.0 to 9.0 with 63.5 mV/pH of the slope, these results are in correlation with the theoretical redox reaction of a reversible, two proton-coupled with two-electron process [68].

Further, the anodic and cathodic peak currents of RF increase with increasing the pH from 3.0 to 7.0, after reaching a maximum current at pH 7.0, the oxidation and reduction peak currents start to decrease from 7.0 to 9.0. The lower current response of RF is due to the RF degradation in alkaline solution. The maximum oxidation and reduction peak currents are observed for RF at the potential of – 0.39 and – 0.47 V in 0.1 M PBS of pH 7.0, which is better known as physiological pH for practical application of real sample analysis. The effect of pH on the electrochemical behavior of RF was also investigated with SWV analysis. Fig. 12 depicts SWV responses of 0.2 µM of RF at Sn/Cs/PGE, the buffer pH was varied from 3.0 to 9.0 and the results are in good correlation with the CV responses. In order to acquire maximum efficiency and stability of electrode/analyte, the pH 7.0 (physiological media) was chosen for further electrochemical analysis towards the electrocatalytic sensing of RF at Sn/Cs/PGE.

The electrochemical redox mechanism of RF on Sn/Cs/PGE modified electrode was studied using scan rate effect by cyclic voltammetry technique. Fig. 13 A illustrates I vs. E curves of RF at Sn/Cs/PGE, the scan rates were varied from 10 to 100 mV s−1. While increasing the scan rate, the oxidation and reduction peak currents also gradually increased. Fig. 13B illustrated, a good linear correlation between the anodic/cathodic peak currents and the square root of scan rates with the correlation coefficient of 0.9960 and 0.9970. The scan rate effect confirms that the electrochemical redox mechanism of RF was diffusion controlled at the tin incorporated chitosan-coated PGE [69].

In order to investigate the maximum sensitivity of Sn/Cs/PGE towards the electrochemical determination of RF, different electroanalytical methods were recorded with the same concentration of RF in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) and the other experimental parameters were kept constant. Fig. 14 depicts the voltammograms of (a) LSV, (b) DPV and (c) SWV of 2.0 µM RF at Sn/Cs/PGE in 0.1 M of PBS (pH 7.0), the oxidation peak current of RF is much higher in SWV analysis compared to that of other voltammetric techniques. So, the SWV technique was chosen for the further electrochemical detection of RF at Sn/Cs/PGE, due to its higher sensitivity.

The sensing performance of RF at Sn/Cs/PGE modified sensor matrix was analyzed by the SWV technique. SWV was performed in 0.1 M PBS, pH 7.0 containing various concentrations of RF at Sn/Cs/PGE and the results were depicted in Fig. 15 A. It was clearly seen that, by increasing the concentration of RF from 10 to 1200 nM, the corresponding oxidation current responses also gradually increases. However, the oxidation peak potential of – 0.38 V vs Ag/AgCl reference electrode is not shifted on the increasing concentration of RF, indicates that the electrode matrix doesn’t undergo any passivation and stable for the wide linear range of RF concentration. Fig. 15B depicts that the linear correlation plot of RF concentration vs anodic peak current, the linearity regression equation and the correlation coefficients are ipa (µM) = 0.0406 (µM) RF + 0.8131 and 0.9931, respectively. Further, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as stated in earlier reports [70,71] and the value of LOD for RF at Sn/Cs/PGE was 5.56 nM. The calculated LOD value of RF is lower than that of previously reported articles and the comparative experimental parameters of the previous articles with our present work were tabulated in Table 1 .

Besides the sensitivity of the electrochemical sensor, the other sensor parameters, such as selectivity, stability and reproducibility also have to be investigated. The selectivity of the electrode towards RF was evaluated by interference analysis using SWV analysis, for that some common interfering ions and coexisting biomolecules were added in 0.1 M PBS pH 7.0 containing 2.0 µM of RF and the results are shown in Table S1. The change in the oxidation peak current of RF is very low in the presence of interfering ions, indicated that the influence of interference is very low in the short scanning potential range of – 0.7 to – 0.1 V. The interfering biomolecules, such as uric acid, ascorbic acid, dopamine and tyrosine were not electrochemically active in the scanned potential range [76,77]. These results suggest that the fabricated electrode sensor was not affected by interferences and have better selectivity towards RF. The long-lasting stability of the electrode sensor was evaluated by storing the fabricated electrode in a vacuum desiccator for 15 days and the change in the SWV response of RF oxidation was meager. The stability on the anodic peak current of RF at the fabricated electrode was evaluated by recording 30 cycles of continuous cyclic voltammograms (Fig. S1), the change in the anodic peak current of RF is very low, indicating the better stability of the fabricated sensor. For the evaluation of reproducibility, five different electrodes were fabricated independently in the same experimental condition and analyzed for the electrochemical detection of RF using the SWV technique. The SWV responses of RF oxidation were similar for all the five independently modified electrodes, indicates the good reproducibility of the fabricated electrode sensor. The above evaluations suggest the better operational selectivity, stability and reproducibility of the fabricated sensor towards the electrochemical detection of RF without electrode fouling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of RF in pharmaceutical and nutritional samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of the fabricated electrode sensor towards the detection RF was investigated, for that medicinal samples containing RF, such as riboflavin, B complex and milk powder were analyzed using SWV analysis. The SWV responses shown in Table S2 depict that the recovery ratio of RF from the medicinal and food samples are 98.1 and 103.2%, which is an acceptable relative standard deviation. This result supports that the fabricated Sn/Cs/PGE electrode has the potential to be used as a sensing material for the electrochemical determination of RF in medicinal and food samples.

We have successfully fabricated the tin incorporated chitosan-coated pencil graphite electrode by a controllable, convenient and simple immersion method, low cost, easily available and reusable pencil graphite leads as an electrode. The surface modification of the fabricated electrode was confirmed by FTIR, SEM, EDX, elemental mapping, CV and EIS analysis and the application of the fabricated sensor were investigated towards the detection of RF using CV and SWV analysis. The tin incorporation on chitosan polymer matrix coating on the PGE surface was dramatically enhanced the redox peak currents of RF, which inferred the better electrocatalytic activity of the Sn/Cs/PGE compared with that of other modified electrode. CV and SWV results display that the sensor can be used for the detection of RF in a wide pH range of 3.0 to 9.0 and gives maximum current response at neutral physiological pH. Scan rate results suggest that the RF redox reaction at Sn/Cs/PGE is diffusion controlled, further the fabricated electrode possesses better sensitivity, selectivity, stability and reproducibility. The obtained LOD value is 5.56 nM in 10 to 1200 nM of linear range. The fabricated sensor possesses practical feasibility in medicinal and food sample analysis, these inferences, suggest the proposed sensor is highly applicable for the detection of RF in medicine and food industries.
N. S. performed all the experiments, electrochemical and mechanistic studies and analyzed the data. N. S. and V. R. conceived the concept, design the project and prepared this manuscript.
